title: Smoke on Cotton
stainChemistry: Smoke residue (soot) on cotton is three problems: (1) carbon particulate lodged mechanically in the fiber, (2) tar / VOC film coating the fibers and carrying odor, (3) for fire smoke, acidic gases that can weaken the fiber if left in prolonged contact. Tobacco smoke adds nicotine yellowing (alkaloid + tar). The #1 home failure is wet-rinsing soot before vacuuming it off — water turns carbon particulate into a smeared gray paste that embeds in the weave. Mechanical removal first, then solvent for tar, then surfactant for residue.
whyThisWorks: Vacuum off dry soot first (NO rubbing — mechanical lift only). Neutral-pH surfactant handles the water-soluble tar film + residue after the carbon is out. Solvent tamp (mineral spirits / POG) clears any bitumen-heavy soot from fire smoke. Airing + sunlight handle the VOC odor that chemistry can't. Hot wash kills any lingering bacteria in organic smoke sources.
defaultAssumption: Smoke on cotton is escalation-prone depending on source: house-fire smoke = pro (often with restoration company). Campfire / BBQ / cigarette = manageable DIY. Vape residue is usually lighter. Clarify source first.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Vacuum with HEPA filter, dust mask
spottingProtocol.instruction: SAFETY FORK: house-fire smoke damage = STOP, don't DIY. The soot is toxic (synthetic-combustion byproducts) and improper cleaning makes it worse. Call a fire-restoration company. Cigarette / campfire / BBQ / vape smoke = proceed with DIY. Put on a dust mask. Vacuum the soot off with a HEPA-filter vacuum — go outdoors or in a well-ventilated space. Do NOT rub, do NOT wet-rinse, do NOT shake the fabric inside. Multiple vacuum passes in different directions.

spottingProtocol.technique: Surfactant pass on residual tar film
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD, work with a soft bristle brush. Handles the water-soluble tar + nicotine residue. Rinse cool from the back. Repeat if staining remains.

spottingProtocol.technique: POG / mineral spirits for bitumen-heavy smoke
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or mineral spirits on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: For fire-smoke or heavy smoker residue with visible tar film: tamp POG / mineral spirits on a white cloth. Ventilate — flammable. Flip cloth frequently.
For residual nicotine-yellow on white cotton: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Skip on dark cotton. One oxidizer at a time.

spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Full wash cycle plus outdoor airing
spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder hot per care label. Air-dry outdoors in sunlight when possible — UV + fresh air clear residual VOC odor better than machine drying. Smoke odor can persist into the fabric's fiber matrix and may need 2–3 wash + air-dry cycles to fully clear.

steps: 1. Source check. Fire-smoke = fire-restoration pro. Tobacco / campfire / BBQ = DIY.
2. HEPA vacuum off dry soot — outdoors, mask on.
4. POG / mineral spirits for heavy tar residue.
6. Hot wash per care label + sun-dry for VOC odor.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry; sun during dry
warnings: House-fire smoke damage = restoration pro only, NOT a DIY — toxic + safety
Vacuum FIRST, wet chemistry SECOND — water on dry soot = gray smear
Ventilation + mask — soot is respiratory irritant
Odor may need multiple wash + sun cycles

homeSolutions.agent: Was This House-Fire Smoke? Stop Here
homeSolutions.instruction: If the smoke damage is from a HOUSE FIRE, STOP. Do not try to DIY-clean. Fire smoke contains combustion byproducts from plastics, electronics, furniture — many of which are toxic, and improper home cleaning can spread the contamination to the rest of your wardrobe. Call a fire-restoration company. Cigarette smoke, campfire / BBQ smoke, vape residue, candle soot = proceed below. When in doubt, pro.

homeSolutions.agent: Vacuum Off Soot BEFORE Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Take the garment outside or to a well-ventilated garage. Put on a dust mask — soot particles are respiratory irritants. Vacuum the fabric thoroughly with your highest-quality vacuum (HEPA filter if possible). Go over it multiple times in different directions. DO NOT rub the fabric, DO NOT shake it indoors, and absolutely DO NOT wet-rinse dry soot — water turns loose carbon into a gray smear that embeds permanently.

Now that the dry soot is off: work a generous amount of Dawn dish soap into the remaining stain with a soft toothbrush. Dawn handles the residual tar film + nicotine yellowing that the vacuum left behind. Rinse warm. Repeat if color persists.

homeSolutions.agent: Heavy Tar / Smoker Residue? Mineral Spirits on a Rag
homeSolutions.instruction: If the fabric came out of a heavy-smoker environment and has a visible yellow-brown tar film: dab mineral spirits (paint thinner) on a clean white rag, press onto the spot from the BACK. Ventilate — flammable. Flip cloth frequently. Then repeat the Dawn step to clean up the solvent residue.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Mineral spirits are flammable. Ventilate. No open flame.

homeSolutions.instruction: For residual yellow tint on white cotton from nicotine / tar: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.instruction: Launder hot per care label. Hang outside in fresh air and sunlight — UV + ventilation handle the VOC odor that chemistry can't. For heavily smoke-exposed garments, this may take 2–3 wash + outdoor-dry cycles to fully clear the smell.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Persistent Odor or Fire Smoke
homeSolutions.instruction: If fabric still smells after 2–3 cycles, or the original source was a fire (see step 1), take to a dry cleaner or fire-restoration company. Tell them: "Smoke on cotton, source [cigarette / campfire / BBQ / fire], home-treated with HEPA vacuum + Dawn + [mineral spirits] + 3% peroxide + hot wash + sun-dry. [Odor persists / visible staining]."

steps: 1. Fire-smoke damage? STOP, call fire restoration.
2. HEPA vacuum dry soot OFF outdoors, mask on. Do NOT wet-rinse soot.
4. Mineral spirits on a rag for heavy tar residue (ventilate).
6. Hot wash + outdoor sun-dry. Multiple cycles may be needed for odor.
7. Escalate if odor persists or source was fire.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: STOP if source is house-fire smoke — restoration pro only. Otherwise stop if odor persists after 2–3 wash + sun-dry cycles.
warnings: FIRE SMOKE = NOT DIY — toxic combustion byproducts
Never wet-rinse dry soot — smears permanently
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Odor may need multiple wash cycles

neverDo: NEVER DIY-clean house-fire smoke — toxic + spreads contamination
Never wet-rinse dry soot — turns into gray smear
Never rub or shake soot-covered fabric indoors
Never work without mask — soot is respiratory irritant
Never open flame near mineral spirits
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates HEPA vacuum, NSD, POG, mineral spirits, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash, sun-dry
Dyed cotton: skip peroxide or inside-seam-test
Heat-durable once soot is mechanically off

materialWarnings: VACUUM dry soot FIRST — wet = smear

escalation.when: Source is house-fire smoke (escalate IMMEDIATELY), odor persists after 2–3 wash + sun-dry cycles, or visible staining plateaus.
escalation.whatToTell: "Smoke on cotton, source [cigarette / campfire / BBQ / fire], home-treated with HEPA vacuum + Dawn + [mineral spirits] + 3% H₂O₂ + hot wash + sun-dry. [Odor persists / visible staining plateau]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner OR fire-restoration company (depending on source)

contextQuestions: What's the smoke source — cigarette, campfire, BBQ, candle, fire damage, vape?
Is the fabric visibly soot-covered or just odor-contaminated?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Source determines everything. Fire smoke = pro. Tobacco = DIY with patience. Vacuum-before-wet is the non-negotiable mechanical step.
